advantages: Simple, fast baseline: closed-form OLS or a few gradient steps on small data; easy to implement without heavy libraries.
Interpretable coefficients: each weight wⱼ describes marginal change in ŷ per unit change in feature xⱼ (when features are scaled comparably).
Convex MSE objective: one global minimum in w (for fixed X); normal equation or gradient descent reliably find the same optimum.
Closed-form solution: w* = (XᵀX)⁻¹Xᵀy when invertible — no hyperparameter search for learning rate in the exact case.
Strong baseline and diagnostic tool: reveals whether relationships are roughly linear before investing in complex models.
MLE consistency under Gaussian noise: minimizing MSE has a clear probabilistic justification (see Maximum Likelihood Estimation).
disadvantages: Linear decision boundary in feature space: cannot capture sharp nonlinear interactions without feature engineering.
Sensitive to outliers under MSE: one extreme y can pull the entire fit — MAE or robust losses behave differently (see Loss Functions).
Collinearity breaks normal equation: redundant features make XᵀX singular; coefficients unstable even if predictions are fine.
Extrapolation risk: linear trends outside training x range often produce unrealistic ŷ (negative prices, impossible scores).
Assumes additive feature effects: no automatic synergy terms unless you add interaction columns manually.
High-dimensional or wide data: inverting XᵀX is O(d³); prefer iterative methods or regularization when d is large.
